title: Best Kites for Intermediate Riders 2026
meta_description: Moving beyond beginner? Our expert picks for intermediate kites in 2026: Duotone Evo, Cabrinha Switchblade & more. Gear up at Surf Store.
body_html:
When Are You an Intermediate Kitesurfer?

You're intermediate when you can: ride upwind consistently, perform basic transitions (heel-to-toe), stay upwind in moderate chop, and self-rescue. You're past the survival phase and ready for a kite that rewards technique rather than just keeping you safe.

What to Look for in an Intermediate Kite

At intermediate level, you want a kite that: responds faster to bar inputs than your beginner kite, delivers more boost for jumping, has a tighter turning radius for transitions, but still offers wide depower as a safety net. You're not ready for a pure C-kite, but you've outgrown the most beginner-friendly models.

Our Top 5 Intermediate Kites

1. Duotone Evo SLS — Best All-Around Progression

The Evo grows with you. At beginner level it's forgiving; at intermediate level its responsiveness and boost reveal themselves. You'll discover the Evo does things your school kite never could — smooth loops, controlled jumps, and confident transitions. This is the kite you'll ride for years.

2. Cabrinha Switchblade — Best for Light Wind Sites

If your home spot averages 12–18 knots, the Switchblade's superior low-end power means more riding days. It generates power earlier than most kites, which is invaluable when you're still building efficiency in your riding.

3. Duotone Dice SLS — Best for Freestyle Path

If you're drawn to unhooked tricks and want to start popping, the Dice teaches you the connected, direct feel of a freestyle kite while remaining forgiving enough to survive mistakes. More demanding than the Evo — but that's the point.

4. Cabrinha Moto X — Best Progression from Moto

If you learned on the Moto, the Moto X is the natural next step — same DNA but more performance. Better boost, tighter turning, and more feedback through the bar.

5. Duotone Rebel SLS — Best for Future Wave Riders

If waves are in your future, start building muscle memory with the Rebel now. Its direct, grunty feel is different from delta kites — learning it early makes the transition to wave riding much smoother.

Size Recommendations for Intermediates

At intermediate level, you can handle slightly smaller kites than when learning. For an 80kg rider, a 9m + 12m two-kite quiver covers 12–30 knots at most European spots. Add a 7m if you ride in 25+ knot conditions regularly.

FAQ

Q: Should I keep my beginner kite?
A: Sell it or keep it as a light-wind option. Your intermediate kite will handle everything your beginner kite did, plus more.

Q: Is an SLS or D/LAB worth the extra cost?
A: At intermediate level, SLS offers the best value — lighter and stiffer than standard without the D/LAB premium. Save D/LAB for when you're advanced.

Q: How long am I intermediate?
A: Most riders spend 1–3 seasons at intermediate level. You become advanced when you can consistently land jumps, ride toeside comfortably, and handle strong wind confidently.

title: Best Windsurf Boards for Beginners 2026
meta_title: Best Beginner Windsurf Boards 2026 — Top Picks
meta_description: Best windsurf boards for beginners: volume guide, top 3 boards, and progression tips. Start windsurfing right with Surf Store.
meta_keywords: beginner windsurf board,learn windsurfing board,first windsurf board
body_html:
What Volume Board Do Beginners Need?

The number one mistake in beginner windsurfing is buying a board that's too small. A board with insufficient volume sinks under your weight, making uphauling impossible and every attempt to stand a frustrating battle with balance. The rule is simple: your body weight in kg + 60–80L = your beginner board volume.

For an 80kg rider, that means 140–160L. Yes, it feels like a barge. Yes, it's slow. But you'll learn to uphaul, sail across the wind, tack, and jibe in days rather than weeks — and that confidence is priceless.

Board Width Matters Too

Width provides lateral stability. Beginners should look for boards at least 75–85cm wide. Narrow boards (under 70cm) are for planing sailors who generate stability through speed. As a beginner, you don't have speed — you need width.

Top 3 Beginner Windsurf Boards

1. Fanatic Gecko — Best Overall Beginner Board

The Gecko is purpose-built for learning. Available in 148L and 156L with an optional centreboard that provides lateral resistance for upwind sailing in light wind. Wide (80cm+), stable, and durable enough to handle the inevitable drops and collisions of learning. Once you progress to planing, you'll outgrow it — but by then you'll know exactly what board you want next.

2. JP Australia Super Ride — Best for Quick Progression

The Super Ride bridges beginner and intermediate — wide enough for learning (75cm) but with a shape that planes well in moderate wind. At 135–160L, it provides stability for uphauling while rewarding technique with early planing once you learn to use the harness and footstraps.

3. Fanatic Blast — Best if You Want to Skip the Beginner Board

If you're athletic, a fast learner, or have board sport experience (surfing, SUP, skateboarding), the Blast at 135–155L lets you learn the basics while already owning a board that performs well at intermediate level. It's less stable than the Gecko for pure beginners, but you won't need to upgrade as quickly.

Should I Learn on an Inflatable Board?

Inflatable windsurf boards exist but we don't recommend them for serious learning. They lack the rigidity needed for efficient sail-to-board power transfer. A proper composite or polyethylene board gives you better feedback and faster progression.

FAQ

Q: Can I learn windsurfing on a SUP board?
A: Some SUP boards accept windsurf rigs, but they're not optimised for sailing. A dedicated windsurf board is significantly better for learning.

Q: How long will I use a beginner board?
A: Typically 1–2 seasons. Once you can plane consistently and use footstraps, you'll want a smaller freeride board.

Q: Should I buy new or used?
A: A quality used beginner board is a smart purchase — you'll outgrow it anyway. Check for delamination, soft spots, and ding repairs before buying.

title: Advanced Kiteboarding Gear Guide 2026
meta_title: Advanced Kite Gear 2026 — Expert Equipment Guide
meta_description: Advanced kiteboarding gear guide: D/LAB kites, carbon boards, foil setups & pro-level accessories. Expert picks from Surf Store.
meta_keywords: advanced kite gear,pro kiteboarding equipment,d/lab kite,carbon kiteboard
body_html:
When You're Ready for Advanced Gear

Advanced gear is for riders who: land jumps consistently, ride comfortably in 25+ knots, have a clear riding style preference (freestyle, wave, big air, or foil), and ride 40+ days per year. If that's you, upgrading from mid-range to premium equipment delivers measurable performance gains.

Advanced Kites: SLS vs D/LAB

At advanced level, construction matters as much as shape. SLS (Stiff Lite Skin) uses carbon-reinforced canopy panels that hold their shape under load — delivering consistent power, faster turning, and better upwind angle than standard construction. D/LAB takes this further with full carbon layup, reducing weight by 15–20% and increasing responsiveness.

The performance difference between SLS and D/LAB is real but subtle. SLS offers the best performance-per-euro for most advanced riders. D/LAB is for riders who compete, ride 80+ days/year, or simply want the absolute best.

Advanced Boards

At advanced level, board choice becomes style-specific:

  • Freestyle: Duotone Jaime D/LAB or Nobile NHP Carbon — stiff, poppy, precision-shaped for trick landings
  • Freeride: Duotone Select SLS — fast, comfortable, excellent upwind for powered sessions
  • Wave: Duotone Whip D/LAB — directional strapless board for surf-style riding
  • Foil: Dedicated foil board 40–60L with centred stance and reinforced foil track

Advanced Foil Setups

If you're not foiling yet, you're missing the most exciting development in kitesurfing. An advanced foil setup uses: a carbon mast (85–95cm), a high-aspect front wing (800–1200cm²) for speed, and a short fuselage for tight turning. The Duotone Spirit system with a carbon Glide mast is our top recommendation.

Accessories That Matter at Advanced Level

Harness: Carbon-shell waist harness (ION Riot Curv, Duotone Apex Curv) — weight savings and load distribution compound over a session.
Impact vest: CE-certified for foiling — a foil crash at speed is no joke.
Helmet: Mandatory for foiling. Optional but recommended for big air.

FAQ

Q: Is D/LAB worth the 40% price premium?
A: For riders who ride 60+ days/year and can feel the difference, yes. For weekend riders, SLS delivers 90% of D/LAB performance at a much lower price.

Q: Should I specialise in one riding style?
A: At advanced level, yes. A dedicated freestyle setup performs dramatically better than an all-round setup for tricks. Same for wave and foil.

Q: What's the biggest upgrade impact?
A: For most riders: a proper harness. Then a performance kite (SLS). Then a sport-specific board.

title: Best Wing Foil Gear for Beginners 2026
meta_title: Best Beginner Wing Foil Gear 2026 — Complete Setup
meta_description: Best wing foil gear for beginners: board, wing, foil sizing & our top starter packages. Start wing foiling right with Surf Store.
meta_keywords: beginner wing foil gear,first wing foil setup,learn wing foil equipment
body_html:
The Beginner Wing Foil Shopping List

Starting wing foiling requires four pieces of equipment. Getting the sizing right is critical — the wrong setup makes learning frustratingly difficult while the right setup has most riders foiling within 10 sessions.

1. Wing — Start Big

Beginners need a wing large enough to generate power in moderate wind without demanding perfect technique. For an 80kg rider: 5.5–6.0m in 12–20 knots. The Duotone Echo is our top beginner pick — easy relaunch, stable power, and forgiving handling. The Duotone Slick is the budget option with similar beginner-friendly characteristics.

2. Board — Volume Is Safety

Your board needs enough volume to support your weight while standing still, practicing wing handling, and attempting first pumps onto the foil. Formula: body weight + 40–50L. For 80kg: a 120–130L board at 78–85cm wide. The Fanatic Sky Wing is our most recommended beginner board — flat deck, wide outline, and a recessed foil track.

3. Foil — Low and Large

Beginners need a large front wing for easy liftoff and a short mast for safe crashes:

  • Front wing: 1500–1800cm² (maximum lift at low speeds)
  • Mast: 65–70cm aluminium (shorter = less dramatic crashes; aluminium = durable during learning)
  • Stabiliser: Standard size (larger stabilisers add pitch stability)

The Duotone Spirit foil with aluminium Glide mast is our recommended starter foil.

4. Safety Gear — Non-Negotiable

Helmet: Always. Foil crashes involve a sharp metal wing underwater.
Impact vest: Strongly recommended. Chest/back protection for falls at speed.
Wing leash: Always. A dropped wing in wind travels fast.

Complete Beginner Packages

Best overall (80kg rider):
Duotone Echo 5.5m + Fanatic Sky Wing 120L + Duotone Spirit 1700 foil + 70cm aluminium mast
Budget: ~€2,500–€3,500

Budget option:
Duotone Slick 6.0m + used high-volume board + aluminium foil set
Budget: ~€1,500–€2,000

FAQ

Q: Can I learn without lessons?
A: Possible but not recommended. 2–3 hours with an instructor saves weeks of trial and error and teaches critical safety skills.

Q: How many sessions to get foiling?
A: 5–15 sessions for most riders with board sport experience. Expect 15–25 without prior experience.

Q: Should I buy used gear?
A: Used boards and foils are a smart buy for learning — you'll upgrade within a season. Buy wings new (bladder condition is hard to assess used).

title: Intermediate Windsurfer — When & What to Upgrade
meta_description: Intermediate windsurfing gear guide: when to upgrade your board and sail, what to buy, and how to progress faster.
meta_keywords: intermediate windsurf gear,windsurf upgrade guide,when to upgrade windsurf board
body_html:
Signs You're Ready to Upgrade

You're intermediate when you can: plane consistently in 15+ knots, use the harness for extended periods, get into footstraps (even if it's not pretty), and waterstart. At this point, your beginner equipment is actively holding you back — it's too heavy, too big, and too slow to develop the next skills.

Upgrade Your Board First

The board upgrade gives the most immediate performance gain. Move from your 140–160L beginner board to a 100–130L freeride board. This smaller volume means: faster planing, tighter jibes, better response to foot pressure, and a board that rewards technique instead of just keeping you floating.

Our picks:

  • Fanatic Blast 115–135L: The best intermediate freeride board — smooth planing, comfortable in chop, and fast enough to keep up with experienced riders
  • Tabou Rocket 115–145L: Excellent value — wide planing surface for early takeoff with a progressive shape that grows with your skills
  • JP Australia Super Ride 110–130L: Slightly sportier — rewards active sailing with responsive rail pressure

Then Upgrade Your Sail

Your beginner sail (probably a wide-luff rotational design) was built for stability, not speed. An intermediate freeride sail like the Gaastra Matrix or NeilPryde Fusion delivers: faster acceleration, better upwind angle, more consistent power in gusts, and a lighter feel that reduces arm fatigue.

Size guide for intermediate upgrade (80kg): keep your existing light-wind sail (7.0–8.0m) and add a smaller performance sail (5.5–6.5m) for stronger wind days.

Don't Upgrade Your Mast — Yet

If your current mast matches the IMCS and luff specifications of your new sail, keep it. Upgrade to a higher carbon percentage (75%+) when you buy your third or fourth sail and have confirmed your preferred sail brand and size range.

FAQ

Q: Should I skip intermediate and buy advanced gear?
A: No — advanced boards under 100L are unstable at low speed. You need planing stability while building technique.

Q: One board or two at intermediate level?
A: One board is enough. A 115–130L freeride covers all conditions. Add a second board only when you specialise (wave, slalom, foil).

Q: How long will intermediate gear last?
A: 2–4 seasons. By then you'll know if you want to specialise in wave, race, freeride, or foil — and can buy accordingly.
